result:
I keep thinking about the way light pools on the kitchen floor at 3:17 p.m.—not 3:15, not 3:20, but that stubborn, in-between minute when the sun slips through the slit between the two buildings across the alley and lands like a spilled yolk, too bright to mop up. It’s there for ninety seconds, maybe a hundred and twenty if the earth is feeling indulgent, and then it’s gone, leaving only the ghost of warmth on the cheap linoleum. I have watched it for three apartments in a row, like I’m trailing a shy pet that refuses to learn my name.

We talk about time as if it’s a line, but inside that rectangle of light it’s a puddle you can step in and out of without getting wet. I once put a spoonful of brown sugar in the spot just to see what would happen; the crystals looked like tiny amber planets dissolving in a private Big Bang. My roommate asked why the floor was sticky. I said I spilled hope. She nodded as if that were a normal answer, which is why we still get along.

When I was small I believed every object had a soul and a schedule. The refrigerator hummed lullabies only the forks could understand. Socks mated for life and grieved in the dark behind the dryer. I used to leave offerings: a marble for the vacuum, a stamp for the flashlight. My mother found the arrangement of pennies under my pillow and assumed I was hoarding allowance. I was actually paying rent to the night-light so it wouldn’t quit and leave me alone with the doorway’s tall rectangle of black. It must have worked; I’m still here, and the bulb only blew once, on the night my grandfather died, as if it had been subletting his pulse.

Sometimes I think the world is made of envelopes. Some are paper, some are skin, some are weather. We’re always ripping them open, looking for the letter that says, “Yes, you matter, here’s the map, skip to the last page.” But the message is almost always just a mirror: your own fingerprints smudged over words you half-remember writing in a dream. I lick the adhesive anyway, because tasting the seal is the closest I get to kissing something that has traveled farther than I have.

I read that astronauts lose height in space—spines stretching in the absence of gravity, then compressing when they come home, arriving shorter than when they left. I wonder if that’s why I keep moving cities: I’m trying to find the altitude where I finally fit. So far I’ve shrunk two centimeters since college. My shoes slap like flip-flops even when they’re laced. I pretend I’m walking in a children’s book where the pages are made of elevator buttons; press one and you’re anywhere, press another and you’re carrying groceries up four flights again, wondering if the milk will sour before you figure out the algorithm for staying.

Last week I saw a woman on the train coloring in a scratch-off ticket with the same feverish concentration a monk gives to mandalas. When all the silver was gone she stared at the revealed zeros like they were a horizon she couldn’t swim to. Then she laughed—one sharp ha!—folded the card into a paper swan, and tucked it behind the plastic seat number as if the vehicle itself might win something on her behalf. I watched commuters sit around it all the way to the end of the line, nobody daring to disturb the origami loser resting like a small white flame. I got off at my usual stop, but I still imagine it riding forever, a mute guardian of unearned luck, wings itching to migrate back to whatever pocket needs hope more than money.

I want to tell her—and the day, and the light, and the spoonful of sugar—that I’m grateful for the rehearsal of almost. Almost is the rehearsal for someday, the dress rehearsal for never, the trampoline under the high wire. It’s what keeps the alphabet one letter away from the word that could finally save us. Meanwhile the kettle whistles, the clock ticks, the alley sun decides whether to visit again. I stand in the kitchen barefoot, waiting for 3:17, ready to pay the rent with whatever small change is clinking in my chest.
